articles.headline: The Reform Convention and the proscription of Pennsylvania's colored citizens
articles.summary: Coverage in the Pennsylvania Freeman (then published as the Philadelphia National Enquirer, edited by Benjamin Lundy for the Eastern District Executive Committee of the Anti-Slavery Society of Pennsylvania) of the Pennsylvania Reform Convention then sitting in Philadelphia and its move to insert the word 'white' into the state constitution, disfranchising the colored citizens of Pennsylvania. The paper condemns the proscription and situates it against the history of Black service and citizenship. A central document of the 1838 disenfranchisement that frames the 1838 Black Metropolis project. No individual Black residents are named in the extract.

articles.headline: Address of the Pennsylvania Abolition Society 'To the People of Color in the State of Pennsylvania'
articles.author: Committee of the Pennsylvania Society for Promoting the Abolition of Slavery
articles.summary: The Pennsylvania Freeman prints an address of a committee of the Pennsylvania Society for Promoting the Abolition of Slavery (the white-led, Quaker-rooted body chartered in 1788 with Benjamin Franklin as its first president) to the People of Color, in the wake of the Reform Convention's vote to disfranchise them. The committee — charged with collecting statistics on the condition of the colored population of the city and districts and offering counsel on their rights and duties — offers consolation, notes the many defenders of Black rights in the Convention (including its president John Sergeant, who recorded a vote against the word 'white'), and urges the community to exemplary conduct. Documents the abolition society's response to the 1838 disenfranchisement of Black Pennsylvanians. Masthead: edited by Benjamin Lundy, published weekly by the Eastern District Executive Committee of the Anti-Slavery Society of Pennsylvania.
